Five Languages, One Problem: Reversing a Linked List

The "reverse a singly linked list" exercise is the lingua franca of programming interviews. It is also, despite its reputation, a surprisingly good lens for comparing language ergonomics. The shape of the problem — a tight loop, three pointer assignments, careful handling of the empty case — exposes how each language handles mutation, ownership, and idiom in microcosm. Here it is in five flavours.

Python

Python's reference semantics make this the simplest of the five. The only thing to watch is that we rebind node on each iteration rather than mutating it in place.

def reverse(head):
    prev = None
    node = head
    while node is not None:
        nxt = node.next
        node.next = prev
        prev = node
        node = nxt
    return prev

JavaScript

Essentially the same shape as Python; the only differences are punctuation and the slightly chunkier null-check.

function reverse(head) {
  let prev = null;
  let node = head;
  while (node !== null) {
    const next = node.next;
    node.next = prev;
    prev = node;
    node = next;
  }
  return prev;
}

Rust

Rust forces the question of ownership to the surface. The clean version uses Option::take to move the tail out without leaving a stale reference behind, which is exactly the move the borrow checker is designed to police.

fn reverse(mut head: Option<Box<Node>>) -> Option<Box<Node>> {
    let mut prev = None;
    while let Some(mut node) = head {
        head = node.next.take();
        node.next = prev;
        prev = Some(node);
    }
    prev
}

Bash

Bash does not have linked lists in any honest sense, but it does have arrays, and reversing one is a useful proxy. The idiom below uses parameter expansion to avoid spawning a subshell, which matters in a hot loop.

reverse_array() {
  local -n src=$1
  local -a out=()
  local i
  for (( i=${#src[@]}-1; i>=0; i-- )); do
    out+=("${src[i]}")
  done
  src=("${out[@]}")
}

SQL

SQL is the most jarring of the five because the question itself has to be reframed. There is no "linked list" — there is a table whose rows reference each other by id. Reversing the chain means inverting which column points to which, expressed as an update over a recursive common table expression.

WITH RECURSIVE chain AS (
    SELECT id, next_id, 0 AS position
    FROM nodes
    WHERE id = (SELECT id FROM nodes WHERE prev_id IS NULL)
    UNION ALL
    SELECT n.id, n.next_id, c.position + 1
    FROM nodes n
    JOIN chain c ON n.id = c.next_id
)
UPDATE nodes
SET next_id = (
    SELECT next.id
    FROM chain cur
    JOIN chain next ON next.position = cur.position - 1
    WHERE cur.id = nodes.id
);

What the exercise reveals

The Python and JavaScript versions look almost identical because both languages model the problem identically: mutable references, garbage collection, no ceremony. Rust is forced to be more explicit, and the result is more verbose but arguably clearer about what is actually happening to the memory. Bash and SQL aren't really doing the same thing at all — they reveal the gap between "the problem as stated" and "the closest equivalent the language supports." That gap is where most real engineering happens.
